subject: Careers
grade_subject_fit: Fits Grade 8 Careers exploration by helping students sort, compare, and interpret possible career pathways through broad cluster categories.
unit_context: Introductory career exploration task focused on organizing jobs into clusters and connecting those clusters to student interests, strengths, and curiosities.
assignment_purpose: Students investigate sample jobs, compare likely cluster fits, and make a justified recommendation about which career cluster currently connects most strongly to their own interests and strengths.
final_evidence_target: An exit-ticket recommendation that names one career cluster, compares it to another cluster, and justifies the decision with personal evidence.
student_task_flow: Learn what career clusters are and how jobs can be grouped by shared skills, work contexts, and interests.
Sort sample jobs into the best-fitting career clusters using evidence from what the work involves.
Compare possible cluster fits and explain why one cluster is the stronger match for selected jobs and personal interests.
Write an exit-ticket recommendation about the career cluster that currently seems like the best fit.
success_criteria: I sorted jobs into clusters using evidence about the work, skills, and setting.
I compared at least one cluster choice clearly instead of only naming an answer.
I justified my final recommendation with interests, strengths, or curiosities that connect to the cluster.
supports_scaffolds: Anchor statements define what a career cluster is and how good sorting works.
A worksheet tip prompts students to think about what the person mainly does, what skills they use, and where the work happens.
Slide examples model how to compare job examples to cluster categories before independent work.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use the anchor statements, guided examples, and teacher check-ins to support one clear cluster comparison before independent reflection.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Sort the sample jobs, justify cluster matches with evidence, and recommend the cluster that feels most relevant right now.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare two plausible cluster fits for one job or explain why a cluster fits better than another using more precise reasoning.

checkpoint_release_logic: Students complete at least one guided cluster sort with evidence before moving into independent job justifications and personal cluster reflection.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep the tone exploratory rather than pressuring students to choose one future path immediately.
Push students to compare cluster fits with evidence instead of relying on stereotypes or surface familiarity.
Use the guided sort to check whether students understand that clusters are broad pathways rather than single jobs.
likely_misconceptions: Students may sort jobs by popularity or stereotype instead of by the actual work, skills, and setting.
Students may treat a career cluster like a single job title instead of a broader pathway category.
Students may describe personal interest without comparing why one cluster fits better than another.
assessment_focus: Accuracy of cluster sorting using evidence
Quality of comparison and recommendation reasoning
Personal justification grounded in interests, strengths, or curiosities

teacher_guide.big_idea: Career clusters help students organize many different jobs into meaningful groups based on common interests, skills, and workplace contexts.
learning_goals: I can explain what a career cluster is.
I can sort jobs into appropriate career clusters using evidence.
I can reflect on which career clusters connect to my interests, strengths, or curiosities.

timing.activity: Launch with quick discussion about jobs students already know and how jobs can be grouped.
Teach the idea of career clusters with clear examples and non-examples.
Guide students through sorting sample jobs into career clusters.
Students complete independent worksheet justifications and personal connections.
Exit ticket reflection and quick debrief.

teacher_notes: Keep the tone exploratory rather than pressuring students to choose one career now.
Use varied examples so students see clusters as broad pathways rather than narrow labels.
Watch for stereotyping and redirect students back to skills, interests, and work contexts.
Keep teacher-only prompts and extension notes out of student-facing materials.

content.subtitle: How can career clusters help us explore possible future pathways?

content.scenario: Think about jobs you already know from your family, community, media, school, or your own interests. How could we group those jobs in a meaningful way?
prompts: What jobs come to mind right away?
Which jobs seem related to each other?
What makes two jobs belong together?

rows: A career cluster is a group of jobs and pathways with related skills, interests, and work settings.
Clusters help us organize many career possibilities into broader areas.
A career cluster is not one job. It is a family of related pathways.

content.right: Health science, skilled trades, education and training
prompts: Which job best matches each cluster?

content.task: Use the worksheet to sort each sample job into the best-fitting cluster and explain your reasoning.

prompts: Which cluster feels most interesting to you right now?
What strengths or interests connect you to that cluster?
Why is it helpful to think in clusters instead of only individual job titles?

anchor: A career cluster is a group of related jobs and pathways.
Good sorting uses evidence about the work, skills, and setting.
worksheet.tip: Ask yourself what the person mainly does, what skills they use, and where the work happens.

questions.q_text: Sort each job into the best-fitting cluster: nurse, carpenter, software developer, educational assistant, chef, welder.
Choose two jobs from the list and explain why they belong in those clusters.
Which career cluster seems most interesting to you right now? Explain what interests, strengths, or curiosities connect you to it.
Why can career clusters be more useful than only thinking about single job titles?

self_check: Did I sort the jobs using evidence?
Did I explain at least one cluster choice clearly?
Did I connect one cluster to my own interests or strengths?
answer_key: Sample clusters: nurse -> health science; carpenter -> skilled trades; software developer -> information technology; educational assistant -> education and training; chef -> hospitality and tourism; welder -> skilled trades

exit_ticket.prompt: Choose one career cluster that interests you right now. Explain why it fits you better than one other cluster, using your interests, strengths, or curiosities as evidence.
success_criteria: I named one career cluster clearly.
I gave a reason connected to my interests, strengths, or curiosities.
I used the exit ticket as my final evidence location.
answer_key: Answers will vary. A strong answer names a cluster and justifies it with personal evidence.
